A number of techniques have been applied to the characterization of the singly dispersed atoms on SACs, including STM, HAADF-STEM, EDX, EELS, XAS, FTIR, SERS,

# Techniques for the characterization of single atom catalysts

Ping Qi[†](#fn1 "Select to navigate to footnote")
a,
Jian Wang[†](#fn1 "Select to navigate to footnote")
a,
Xavier Djitcheua,
Dehua He[![ORCID logo](/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)](http://orcid.org/0000-0003-0027-7002 "Select to open ORCID record for Dehua He (orcid.org/0000-0003-0027-7002) in a new window")b,
Huimin Liu
[![ORCID logo](/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)](http://orcid.org/0000-0001-8258-8263 "Select to open ORCID record for Huimin Liu (orcid.org/0000-0001-8258-8263) in a new window")\*a and

[![ORCID logo](/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)](http://orcid.org/0000-0003-1992-031X "Select to open ORCID record for Qijian Zhang (orcid.org/0000-0003-1992-031X) in a new window")\*a
  
aSchool of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Liaoning University of Technology, Jinzhou 121001, P. R. China
  
bInnovative Catalysis Program, Key Lab of Organic Optoelectronics & Molecular Engineering of Ministry of Education, Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

![ORCID logo](/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)
![ORCID logo](/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)
![ORCID logo](/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)

First published on 5th January 2022

## Abstract

Single atom catalysts (SACs) are a hot research area recently. Over most of the SACs, the singly dispersed atoms are the active sites, which contribute to the catalytic activities significantly compared with a catalyst with continuously packed active sites. It is essential to determine whether SACs have been successfully synthesized. Several techniques have been applied for the characterization of the dispersion states of the active sites over SACs, such as Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), Electron Energy Loss Spectroscopy (EELS), etc. In this review, the techniques for the identification of the singly dispersed sites over SACs are introduced, the advantages and limitations of each technique are pointed out, and the future research directions have been discussed. It is hoped that this review will be helpful for a more comprehensive understanding of the characterization and detection methods involved in SACs, and stimulate and promote the further development of this emerging research field.

## 1 Introduction

Inspired by this remarkable research, SACs have been widely investigated in various catalytic reactions. It has been found that SACs have several advantages.[6–8](#cit6 "Select to navigate to reference") (1) In SACs, the utilization of active center atoms is greatly improved due to their high dispersion.[9–12](#cit9 "Select to navigate to reference") (2) The strong interaction at the active center–support interface of SACs tunes the electronic properties, leading to the tailorable catalytic activity.[13–16](#cit13 "Select to navigate to reference") (3) The highly dispersed atoms on the surface of the catalyst have limited numbers of configurations to adsorb the reactant molecules. Compared with traditional nanoparticle (NP) catalysts, side reactions could be significantly inhibited and the selectivity towards the desired products could be improved over SACs.[17](#cit17 "Select to navigate to references")

Because of these advantages, SACs are widely applied in reactions where a single active site works, such as CO oxidation reaction,[18–20](#cit18 "Select to navigate to reference") hydrogenation reactions[21–23](#cit21 "Select to navigate to reference") and so on. On the contrary, SACs cannot catalyze reactions that require surface ensemble sites,[24,25](#cit24 "Select to navigate to reference") such as hydrocarbon (C ≥ 2) oxidation reaction[26](#cit26 "Select to navigate to references") and hydrogenolysis reaction,[27](#cit27 "Select to navigate to references") in which traditional nanoparticle catalysts have been extensively explored. Consequently, SACs are a promising research direction in specific reactions.

In single atom catalysis, it is particularly important to determine whether SACs have been successfully synthesized. Up to now, several techniques have been utilized to investigate the presence of single atoms (SAs) in SACs. These techniques could be roughly divided into two types. One type includes high resolution scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) and high angle annular dark field scanning transmission electron microscope (HAADF-STEM), which can directly observe the atomic morphology.[28](#cit28 "Select to navigate to references") The other type consists of X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) and surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), which can provide auxiliary evidence for the existence of SAs (the information of SACs that can be obtained by different techniques are listed in [Table 1](#tab1 "Select to navigate to table")). Giving an introduction of these techniques to the readers will give them a clear picture and inspire more studies on the area of SACs. As some of the techniques, such as STM, HAADF-STEM, XAS, FTIR and XPS have already been reviewed somewhere,[28](#cit28 "Select to navigate to references") this review emphasizes on the applications of EDX, EELS and SERS in SACs. We will briefly introduce the principles of these techniques, illuminate the advantages and limitations of each technique, and emphasize the necessity of combining multiple characterization approaches to fully investigate SACs. In addition, ex situ techniques can not give the precise information of SAs on the catalyst surface in reaction atmosphere, because the atomic structure of SACs might undergo transformation. Hence, in situ/operando techniques are also introduced in this review for the determination of the singly dispersed sites over SACs under the working conditions.

| Techniques | Uses | Information obtained | Merits/disadvantages | Typical resolution |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| XAS | Ex situ and in situ operando | Valence state; electronic structure; coordination number | Atomic level sensitivity; in situ recognition of adsorption process; it is sensitive to chemical groups, local structures and spatial coordination; the test time is short and the damage to the sample is small; complex data processing | 0.1 nm |
| STM | Ex situ and in situ operando | Morphology structure; atomic distribution state | Atomic level sensitivity; in situ recognition of adsorption process; the working environment has universal applicability; the result is easily affected by environment | 0.01 nm vertical resolution |
| HAADF-STEM | Ex situ and in situ operando | Atomic distribution state; morphology structure | Atomic level sensitivity; in situ recognition of adsorption process; thick and low contrast specimens can be observed; micro diffraction can be realized; imaging is limited to selected areas | 0.08 nm |
| EDX | Ex situ and in situ operando | Element type analysis | Atomic level sensitivity; in situ recognition of adsorption process; used with HAADF-STEM; low energy resolution | 0.1 nm |
| EELS | Ex situ and in situ operando | Element and chemical state analysis | Atomic level sensitivity; in situ recognition of adsorption process; high electronic structure sensitivity; insensitive to high number atoms | 0.1 nm |
| SERS | Ex situ and in situ operando | Crystal structure; chemical bonding | Atomic level sensitivity; in situ recognition of adsorption process; difficult sample preparation process; only specific elements could be analyzed | 0.1 nm |
| FTIR | Ex situ and in situ operando | Molecular groups; chemical bonding | Atomic level sensitivity; in situ recognition of adsorption process; only specific molecules can be detected | 0.1 nm |
| XPS | Ex situ and in situ operando | Element analysis; chemical state | Atomic level resolution; it can detect detailed electronic structure and geometric information; in situ detection process; the information is not persuasive | 0.1 nm |

## 2 Characterization techniques for evidence of single atoms in SACs

### 2.1 X-ray absorption spectroscopy

XAS was universally utilized to reflect the dispersion of active sites on the surface of SACs.[34](#cit34 "Select to navigate to references") For example, Chen et al. synthesized a SAs Au1/mpg-C3N4 catalyst and used XAS to determine the electronic and geometric structures of the singly dispersed Au sites.[34](#cit34 "Select to navigate to references") The drastic difference between the XANES features for Au1/mpg-C3N4 and Au foil, as well as Au NPs/mpg-C3N4. The result in [Fig. 1a](#imgfig1 "Select to navigate to figure") implied that the Au atoms were possibly singly dispersed on support, the same threshold value (11![[thin space (1/6-em)]](https://www.rsc.org/images/entities/char_2009.gif)919.4 eV) for Au over Au1/mpg-C3N4 and AuCl indicated that the valence of Au on Au1/mpg-C3N4 was +1. The lacking the peak assigned to the Au–Au bond in the r-space spectrum of Au1/mpg-C3N4 further confirmed the singly dispersed state of Au on Au1/mpg-C3N4 ([Fig. 1b](#imgfig1 "Select to navigate to figure")).[35](#cit35 "Select to navigate to references")

![[thin space (1/6-em)]](https://www.rsc.org/images/entities/char_2009.gif)

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [image file: d1ra07799f-f1.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f1_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window") | | |
|  | **Fig. 1**  (a) XANES spectra of Au L3 edge. (b) r-space spectra of Au1/mpg-C3N4 and a series of reference samples. This figure has been reproduced from ref. [35](#cit35 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Wiley-VCH, copyright 2018. |  |

![image file: d1ra07799f-f1.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f1.gif)

Atomic Cu1-N2/HCNS catalyst was prepared by the simple reduction deposition-acid leaching method. Due to the unique atomic structure of Cu in the catalyst, the activity of SAs Cu-N2 is much better than that of Cu-N3. Under the same reaction condition, the catalytic activity of the former was increased by 3.4 times. Cu1-N2/HCNS catalyst afforded higher selectivity and stability than Cu NPs or nanoclusters.[36](#cit36 "Select to navigate to references")

In order to further investigate the electronic properties and coordination environment of Cu1-N2/HCNS, XANES and EXAFS were utilized. The results in [Fig. 2a](#imgfig2 "Select to navigate to figure") indicated outline of Cu K-edge XANES. Cu1-N2/HCNS exhibited an energy absorption edge between Cu+ and Cu2+ standards in the range of E0 (about 8979 eV). The corresponding oxidation state of Cu is +1.5 by linear combination fitting. On the EXAFS spectrum ([Fig. 2b](#imgfig2 "Select to navigate to figure")) of Cu1-N2/HCNS, there was only a peak at 1.9 Å, which revealed the dispersion of single Cu atoms in the whole HCNs matrix.[36](#cit36 "Select to navigate to references")

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [image file: d1ra07799f-f2.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f2_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window") | | |
|  | **Fig. 2**  (a) Cu K-edge XANES spectra. (b) Cu K-edge EXAFS spectra. This figure has been reproduced from ref. [36](#cit36 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Elsevier, copyright 2019. |  |

![image file: d1ra07799f-f2.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f2.gif)

In addition to the examples listed above, XAS could be applied in situ to reveal the structure of SACs under realistic reaction conditions.[37–41](#cit37 "Select to navigate to reference") For instance, Wei et al. used in situ XAS to track the evolution of the catalytic active sites of Co SACs under alkaline hydrogen evolution reaction.[38](#cit38 "Select to navigate to references") They revealed that the unsaturated singly dispersed Co sites was subjected to undergo a structural and valence state evolution under the reaction atmosphere. They would transform into an HO–Co1–N2 moiety upon the adsorption of a hydroxyl group and further involve into H2O–(HO–Co1–N2) by adsorbing water molecules.[38](#cit38 "Select to navigate to references")

The center atoms with different coordination numbers or ligands exhibit different XANES adsorption curves. Consequently, it is the most straightforward and beneficial approach to identify the existence of SAs. XAS and in situ XAS are critical in providing the structural details of SACs for unraveling the reaction mechanisms at an atomic level. Yet, the operation of XAS is quite complex and the analysis/interpretation of XAS data is complicated, which limits the reproducibility of XAS final results.

### 2.2 Scanning tunneling microscopy

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [image file: d1ra07799f-f3.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f3_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window") | | |
|  | **Fig. 3**  Schematic diagram of STM. This figure has been reproduced from ref. [46](#cit46 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Elsevier, copyright 2012. |  |

![image file: d1ra07799f-f3.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f3.gif)

For example, Flytzani-Stephanopoulos et al. utilized high-resolution STM to characterize the distribution of Pt atoms on Cu(111) surface. The results in [Fig. 4a and b](#imgfig4 "Select to navigate to figure") indicated that the Pt atoms are singly and randomly distributed throughout the surface of Cu(111), with most of the Pt atoms on the terraces and near the step edges of Cu(111).[47](#cit47 "Select to navigate to references")

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [image file: d1ra07799f-f4.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f4_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window") | | |
|  | **Fig. 4**  STM and high resolution STM images of Pt1/Cu(111). This figure has been reproduced from ref. [47](#cit47 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Nature Publishing Group, copyright 2018. |  |

![image file: d1ra07799f-f4.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f4.gif)

STM could be upgraded into in situ STM by introducing reactants and/or heat into the characterization system. In situ STM allows the direct observation of the SACs structure under the reaction atmosphere. For instance, in situ STM is applied by Sykes et al. to investigate the structure of Pt1/Cu2O in CO oxidation reaction at low temperatures. The experimental and simulated STM images ([Fig. 5a and b](#imgfig5 "Select to navigate to figure")) demonstrated that CO was adsorbed onto Pt SAs.[48](#cit48 "Select to navigate to references")

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [image file: d1ra07799f-f5.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f5_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window") | | |
|  | **Fig. 5**  (a) and (b) Experimental and simulated in situ STM images of Pt1/Cu2O in CO oxidation reaction. This figure has been reproduced from ref. [48](#cit48 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Nature Publishing Group, copyright 2018. |  |

![image file: d1ra07799f-f5.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f5.gif)

STM has been applied to investigate the singly dispersed atoms on the surface of SACs. STM could be manipulated under many working conditions, including ultra-high vacuum, gaseous atmosphere, and liquid atmosphere, at a temperature range of 0–1273 K.[49](#cit49 "Select to navigate to references") The vertical resolution of STM is 0.1 nm. However, it could not be used to recognize the embedded atoms.

### 2.3 High angle annular dark field scanning transmission electron microscopy

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [image file: d1ra07799f-f6.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f6_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window") | | |
|  | **Fig. 6**  Schematic illustration of HAADF-STEM. This figure has been reproduced from ref. [50](#cit50 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Wiley-VCH, copyright 2021. |  |

![image file: d1ra07799f-f6.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f6.gif)

The bright spots in the image always reflect the real atoms. More electrons are diffracted by elements of higher atomic numbers, which then appears brighter in the HADDF-STEM imaging. HADDF-STEM could be utilized to investigate catalyst properties.[50](#cit50 "Select to navigate to references")

Regarding SACs, if there is a great discrepancy between the atomic number of the singly dispersed atom and support,[51–56](#cit51 "Select to navigate to reference") singly dispersed atoms could be distinguished by HAADF-STEM. The X-rays produced by electronic attacking of catalyst surface are collected by EDX detector and electrons passing through the catalyst are collected by the EELS detector. Multiple devices are synergistically utilized to explore the atomic states of SAs. The details of EDX and EELS will be minutely described later.

Fe SACs were synthesized by pyrolyzing coordinated polymer strategy. [Fig. 7](#imgfig7 "Select to navigate to figure") displayed the dispersion of Fe SAs in Fe-C/Al2O3 catalyst. The HAADF-STEM image straightforwardly demonstrated that Fe existed in the state of SAs. Compared with Fe nanoparticle catalyst, Fe SAC has noteworthy advantages. Fe SAC possesses not only the improved atomic utilization efficiency but also stronger interaction between the active sites Fe and matrix Al2O3. The strong interaction leads to (1) the change of the chemical state of Fe atoms and (2) the enhanced adsorption capacity for reactant gases. In addition, the limited number of configurations between the active sites Fe and reactant gases inhibits the occurrence of side reactions.[57](#cit57 "Select to navigate to references")

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [image file: d1ra07799f-f7.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f7_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window") | | |
|  | **Fig. 7**  HAADF-STEM image of Fe-C/Al2O3. This figure has been reproduced from ref. [57](#cit57 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Elsevier, copyright 2021. |  |

![image file: d1ra07799f-f7.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f7.gif)

In situ HAADF-STEM is an approach to characterize the dispersion state of the active sites and visualize the dynamics of SACs under reaction atmosphere.[41,57–63](#cit41 "Select to navigate to reference") For instance, Li et al. visualized the transformation of noble metal particles (Pd, Pt, and Au) into singly dispersed atoms at a temperature above 900 °C in an inert atmosphere via in situ HAADF-STEM ([Fig. 8a and b](#imgfig8 "Select to navigate to figure") are the images for Pt NPs before and after introducing heat and inert atmosphere into the HAADF-STEM chamber).[64](#cit64 "Select to navigate to references")

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [image file: d1ra07799f-f8.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f8_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window") | | |
|  | **Fig. 8**  (a) In situ HAADF-STEM image of Pt NPs (short for Pt-NPs in the image) and (b) Pt SACs (short for Pt-SAs in the image). This figure has been reproduced from ref. [64](#cit64 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Nature Publishing Group, copyright 2018. |  |

![image file: d1ra07799f-f8.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f8.gif)

HAADF-STEM could directly provide information on the local chemistry of a catalyst at the atomic scale. It is convenient to determine whether SACs have been successfully synthesized or not. However, HAADF-STEM could not provide overall information of SACs, since the images are only limited to the selected areas.

In situ HAADF-STEM could be utilized to characterize the dispersion state of the active sites and visualize the dynamics of SACs under the reaction atmosphere. Due to the operation limitation of HAADF-STEM, only a trace amount of reactant gases is introduced into the operation chamber to mimic the reaction atmosphere and there is still a long way to go to obtain the information under realistic reaction atmosphere.

### 2.4 Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy

In the case that a high-resolution image is needed, the electronic beam of higher energy is required to attack the catalyst surface to generate the X-ray. The inner electrons of different elements are excited by electronic beams to emit characteristic X-rays. The characteristic spectrum can be recorded by the EDX detector. Then the type of elements in the catalyst are determined by the wavelength of the characteristic emitting X-ray and the content of elements are determined by comparing the intensity with the standard elemental spectrum.[66](#cit66 "Select to navigate to references") Apart from that, EDX mapping could reflect the spatial distribution of the elements. EDX is used to identify the elements in catalysts and STEM could determine whether they are SAs or not.

Li et al.'s work is a typical example. They adopted EDX mapping analysis and HAADF-STEM to characterize the single dispersion state of Pt on Pt1/Ni(OH)x.[67](#cit67 "Select to navigate to references") Defect-rich Ni(OH)x nanoboards was synthesized and fabricated Pt onto Ni(OH)x via a simple wet impregnation method was depicted in [Fig. 9a and b](#imgfig9 "Select to navigate to figure"). EDX mapping analysis suggested that the Pt species were evenly dispersed on Ni(OH)x ([Fig. 9c](#imgfig9 "Select to navigate to figure")). HAADF-STEM measurements verified that the Pt species were isolatedly dispersed as single atomic sites ([Fig. 9d](#imgfig9 "Select to navigate to figure")). The EDX mapping analysis and HAADF-STEM characterization results indicated that Pt atoms were singly dispersed on Ni(OH)x nanoboards.

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [image file: d1ra07799f-f9.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f9_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window") | | |
|  | **Fig. 9**  (a) TEM image of Ni(OH)x nanoboards. Scale bar, 50 nm. (b) HRTEM image of a Ni(OH)x nanoboard. Scale bar, 20 nm. (c) EDX mapping analysis of Pt1/Ni(OH)x. Scale bar, 100 nm. (d) HAADF-STEM image of Pt1/Ni(OH)x. The singly dispersed Pt atoms are marked by yellow circles. This figure has been reproduced from ref. [67](#cit67 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Nature Publishing Group, copyright 2018. |  |

![image file: d1ra07799f-f9.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f9.gif)

The work of Zhang et al. is another representative example. HAADF-STEM and EDX are utilized to determine the morphology and composition of Ni-MoS2. The results are displayed in [Fig. 10](#imgfig10 "Select to navigate to figure"). HAADF-STEM image ([Fig. 10a](#imgfig10 "Select to navigate to figure")) and corresponding element mapping image ([Fig. 10b](#imgfig10 "Select to navigate to figure")) indicated that Mo (green), S (yellow) and Ni (red) are uniformly distributed on the surface of the catalyst, suggesting Ni was successfully introduced into MoS2 surface. HAADF-STEM image ([Fig. 10c](#imgfig10 "Select to navigate to figure")) of Ni-MoS2 indicated that single Ni atoms (represented by red circles in [Fig. 10d](#imgfig10 "Select to navigate to figure")) were dispersed on the MoS2 plane. The red cycles in [Fig. 10d](#imgfig10 "Select to navigate to figure") represented that the single Ni atoms occupied the position of Mo atoms. In this work, the SACs were studied synergetically via HAADF-STEM and EDX. EDX analysis of the selected region proved that there are only Ni, Mo and O in this region, while HAADF-STEM intuitively demonstrated that MoS2 surface was replaced by other atoms. Therefore, it is fully proved that Ni existed in the form of SAs on MoS2 surface.[68](#cit68 "Select to navigate to references")

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [image file: d1ra07799f-f10.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f10_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window") | | |
|  | **Fig. 10**  (a) HAADF-STEM of single-atom Ni-doped MoS2; (b) HAADF-STEM image of Ni-MoS2 and corresponding elemental mapping images of Mo, S and Ni; HAADF-STEM image of Ni-MoS2 (c) and magnified domain (d) with red dashed rectangle in (c); (e) EDX spectrum of the Ni-MoS2 sample. This figure has been reproduced from ref. [68](#cit68 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Elsevier, copyright 2021. |  |

![image file: d1ra07799f-f10.tif](/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f10.gif)

# Characterization of single-atom catalysts by EELS and EDX spectroscopy

### Affiliations

# Characterization of single-atom catalysts by EELS and EDX spectroscopy

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

Fitted with a field emission source, aberration-corrected optics and an energy-dispersive X-ray detector of large solid angle, a modern analytical TEM can generate a current density high enough to chemically identify a single metal atom within a fraction of a second, if the atom remains stationary within the electron probe. However, atom motion will occur if the atomic binding energy is too low, the specimen temperature too high, or the electron accelerating voltage above a certain threshold. We discuss such motion in terms of thermal diffusion, beam-induced sputtering and beam-assisted surface migration. Calculations based on a Rutherford-scattering approximation suggest that when atomic displacement is possible, it drastically reduces the analytical signal and signal/noise ratio. For certain elements, electron energy-loss spectroscopy (EELS) provides a higher detectability than energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) but suffers from the same problem of atomic displacement.

**Keywords:**
Adatom motion; Catalyst analysis; Electron energy-loss spectroscopy; Energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry; STEM.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX) identifies elemental composition by detecting characteristic X-rays emitted from sample atoms, providing insights into the distribution of single atoms in catalysts.
Electron Energy Loss Spectroscopy (EELS) examines the electronic structure and chemical states of single atoms by measuring energy losses of electrons after interacting with the sample.
Extended X-ray Absorption Fine Structure (EXAFS) analyzes the local coordination environment around single atoms, revealing distances and types of neighboring atoms in catalysts.
X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) measures the binding energies of core electrons to determine oxidation states and electronic configurations of elements within single atom catalysts.
